sentences.text: Yes, I'm Tom Simon.
I was called Somogyi before my name change, which took place, I believe, in 1966.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was born in Hungary-- Pecs, Hungary.
It's a provincial town in the area called Transdanubia, I suppose, the southern part near the Yugoslav-- the former Yugoslav border.
I was born in 1933-- to be exact, April 14, 1933.
And I lived in Pecs intermittently, that is, when I was not deported until age 16, which was in 1949.
Yes, before the war, my family consisted of five members-- my father and mother, my older sister, Alice, and my twin brother and I.
My twin brother's name is Peter.
After the war, only three of us survived-- that's my father and my twin brother and I.
My mother and sister perished in Auschwitz.
Her maiden name was Elizabeth-- actually in Hungarian, would be Erzsebet.
And her last name was Eisner, E-I-S-N-E-R.
Yes.
They didn't all live in Pecs.
Some of them lived in surrounding villages and towns.
I did have a fairly large family, both on my father's side and on my mother's side.
My father had, I believe, three or four brothers and, and a sister.
And on my mother's side-- my mother had two sisters and two brothers.
And, of course, they all were married and had children.
So I had a lot of cousins, first cousins before the war.
Very few of them survived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of my first cousins, actually, got to the States and got out of Nazi-occupied Europe before the Holocaust.
But of the ones that did not, I believe only one cousin survived.
I'm trying to remember.
I believe there was only one cousin who survived.
Yes, of course.
We, we were close.
I mean, transportation in Hungary in those days, in provincial towns like Pecs-- it wasn't all that interurban.
Transportation was not as it is today.
And so we didn't get together as often as you would do today.
But on special occasions, yes, we did, and birthdays and other celebrations and holidays, possibly.
We did have a-- I had a fairly religious upbringing, in the sense that my household was kosher, strictly kosher.
My late mother kept a kosher household.
But I-- the way I remember it, when we were away from home, we would not be strictly kosher.
But at home, yes, we would.
And of course, we had Passover dishes and, of course, all the other trappings of kosher households.
Well, it's an-- as I mentioned, it's a provincial town in southern Hungary, southwestern Hungary.
At the time-- the time we're talking about, before the war, it had a population of about 75,000.
It's a university town.
In fact, I believe it's the first university in Hungary that was established there in the 14th century.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in that sense, it was kind of a cultural center.
It was a pleasant little town, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- sort of next to a mountain range, which probably in Canada would be called hills, but in, in Hungary, it was a mountain.
And, and it was, yeah, pleasant.
The climate was quite pleasant.
It was fairly warm-- not Mediterranean, but moderate.
The Jewish community before the war, I believe, was numbered about 4,500 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out of a population, as I said, about 75,000.
No, it wasn't a very large Jewish, you know, community.
But it was fairly well off.
My, my own family-- my father was certainly middle-class.
I mean, we were a middle-class family.
I wouldn't consider ourselves wealthy people.
But we were certainly well off.
He had a business.
And he-- we certainly didn't lack any of the-- of the essentials of life.
He was selling automotive parts, which, of course, back in the-- when they started that business in, in the 1920s.
And there were very few automobiles in Hungary in the 1920s.
But he was one of the first.
In fact, I think he was the first in town selling cars and, and, and, and automotive parts.
He had a Ford-- he was a representative of Ford.
It was, I think, the European Ford.
It wasn't the American Ford.
And other-- he had other representations as well.
So he did very well.
And unfortunately, of course, his business was confiscated, taken away, first, by the Germans, and then, by the Communists after the war.
So he lost his business twice.
And it's-- he never really-- after losing his business, he never really became-- you know, he did OK.
But he was not-- never the same anymore, as far as financially or, or business-wise.
As a young boy, we went to a-- both my brother and I, and my sister, too, of course-- we went to a Jewish elementary school, the only Jewish elementary school in Pecs.
And that was a four year.
The school system there was, of course, four years elementary school and then what was called 'gymnasium,' which is the equivalent of a high school in Canada, I suppose.
The eight grades of high school, of gymnasium.
So we went-- after completing the four-year elementary school, Jewish elementary school, we were enrolled in, in a private gymnasium run by the Cistercian Order of priests.
That was considered the best high school in Pecs.
And it wasn't very pleasant in some ways because obviously, being Jewish in a-- in a-- in a Catholic school, strictly Catholic school, it had its disadvantages.
And we were not, in fact-- we were not, in fact, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] proper students.
We were-- we were considered external students to, to school because we were not allowed to be enrolled as regular students in, in, in, in that gymnasium.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I recall, at the end of the first term, or first-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excuse me-- at the end of the first term-- [DRINKS] the first year of school 1944, just before we were deported, we had to go to exams in that school with our yellow star badge on our Hungarian national uniform, which was pretty incongruous, [LAUGHS] to say the least.
And I recall that I think the priests were more embarrassed than we were, seeing us.
I'm not sure if they were embarrassed to see us with the yellow Star of David.
I think they were embarrassed because the yellow Star of David was on the national uniform of the school.
And, and that, that was-- that, that was, I-- I think, very objectionable to them.
But again, I-- just like in any group of people, you had some priests who were more sympathetic.
And there were some who were less sympathetic to us.
Some were probably almost openly anti-Semitic.
Others were very, very kind.
And, and so you, you can't really generalize in even in a small group as, as maybe a dozen or two dozen teachers.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes, of course.
There were-- there were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were instances when there were fights and-- in that school, when we were harassed by other students because of being Jewish.
I recall one instance when we were studying history.

And our teacher was actually talking about-- I, I think it was the, the, the Roman conquest of Jerusalem in AD 67, and mentioned that, at that point, the Jews were all dispersed around the world as a result of the Roman conquest.
And one of the kids in, in, in the class made a loud comment, saying, 'unfortunately.' And the teacher responded, [BACKGROUND NOISE] I'm not interested in anybody's opinion.
So in that regard, the teacher was, you know, quite-- I appreciated that.
Yes, some.
I don't think they were very close to us.
But, yes, we were friendly with some of them.
Some, as I say, again, just like with the teachers, some of the students were quite, quite sympathetic to us and friendly.
And others were not.
And there's another-- a point, of course, here to mention is that some of the students were actually non-Catholic but non-Jewish.
We had Protestant-- a few Protestant kids in the class.
And they were treated sometimes as badly, or almost as badly, as the Jews.
I remember, again, history class when they were taught-- when the priest was teaching about Reformation.
Those, those kids were-- they were uncomfortable because, obviously, the Reformation was taught from the point of view of the Catholic church.
And those Protestant children were not very comfortable in class.
So it wasn't just the Jews, but some of the Protestants.
There weren't too many-- I think there were a couple of Protestants in the class, one Calvinist and one Lutheran.
But-- so it wasn't just us.
But there were only two Jews.
And that was my brother and I, my twin brother and I.
Were you exceptionally close with your brother?
Yes, of course.
I mean, we were twins.
We were-- We are fraternal twins.
We're not identical twins.
So biologically, we're no, no closer than brothers.
We're, we're basically brothers who were born on the same day, on the same date, biologically speaking.
But, yes, we, of course, were close.
And we went through the same kind of life and, and, and horrors and, and difficulties throughout-- I mean, we were not actually separated.
I was never separated from my brother up to age-- oh, I think, the first time it was in Israel and we were about-- we were about 19 or 20.
Until that time, we always stayed together.
I mean, we slept in the same room.
We, we went to the same school.
We went to-- and when we went to Israel, we were in the same kibbutz.
So we-- obviously, we were close.
Well, obviously not, because, I mean, we did separate.
But I think we probably weren't as close as identical twins would be.
We do have different temperaments, I suppose.
And we do have different outlook sometimes on life.
And, and we-- we're not-- we're not identical in our views and our-- and our thinking.
But, but we are close.
I mean, I owe my life to him.
And he owes my life-- his life to me.
And I'll get to that, I suppose, later.
Before the war?
No, we were too small to-- no, I mean, we weren't-- other than going to the Jewish elementary school, we weren't active.
And there wasn't-- no, I shouldn't say that.
There was a, a scouting, a Jewish scouting movement that-- I suppose you would call them Cubs today-- before the war that we were involved in marginally.
We weren't too active.
But we were, I recall, in this scouting movement.
There was a scouting-- scout group, I supposed you would call it, that were made up of Jewish kids.
Mm-hmm.
My-- the-- during the Holocaust, or at the time of the deportation, I, I only had one grandmother that was still alive.
Both my grandfathers and the other grandmother were dead by that time.
The only-- my maternal grandmother was alive.
And she was actually deported together with us.
Yes, of course.
Yes, I, I wasn't that young.
I mean, when the war broke out, it so happens I think it was the first day of my school, September 1, 1939.
I had just started elementary school.
Yes, we did know.
I mean, I did have a-- an-- some kind of notion about the war breaking out.
But, but I recall-- and, and this, this shows the power of the German propaganda-- I recall playing with toys where I was rooting for the German Army.
I mean, basically saying, now, Germany is, is, is-- you know, the German troops are moving forward.
And, and then-- I didn't realize what was going on, but-- at, at least when I was six years old, or seven.
Later on, of course, I, I, I did smarten up.
I recall my father listening to the BBC.
Of course, that had to be done in, in, in secret because that was punishable by-- well, by, by probably a prison term, if, if you were caught listening to the BBC.
But he was listening to it.
And, and we had to be very careful about it.
I recall my father's sister, who came to see us at some point and stay with us.
She lived in Vienna.
And after the Anschluss, she managed to escape.
Her husband was dead by that time.
And her children weren't there.
But she managed to escape to Hungary.
And she actually came to live with us for a number of years.
And it was all done illegally.
Her name was Emma Strauss.
That was her, her married name-- Strauss, S-T-R-A-U-S-S.
Her maiden name was, of course, well, the same as my, my father, Somogyi.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she lived with us for a number of years.
And she really hoped at the time, of course, that she escaped from the horrors.
But, but she didn't.
I mean, she was deported at the same time we were deported.
And she never came back.
Oh, yes.
I mean, I knew about the Numerus Clausus, which was the law that was enacted in Hungary about limiting the university students, Jewish university students.
It was actually enacted as early as-- I, I think it was in 1933 or thereabouts.
I may be wrong about the date.
But I understand it, it anti-dates just about any similar laws, even in Germany.
As far as I know, the Numerus Clausus was a very, very virulent type of anti-Semitic law in Hungary.
Hungarians were right in the forefront of anti-Semitism, unfortunately.
Well, obviously, I was not a university student.
So I, I, I didn't experience it firsthand.
But I knew about it and other laws.

Yes, there were-- there were-- the Jews were limited in, in doing things in, in-- probably starting in the '30s and, and getting gradually worse in the '40s, even before the Germans occupied the country.
I mean, it was no bed of roses.
But relatively speaking, we were much better off than Jews in, say, Poland or, or Czechoslovakia or, or the Baltic states.
There's no question about it.
We did not-- I certainly did not know, but I don't think my parents knew at the time what went-- what went on in-- on in Poland and, and-- about concentration camps.

I understand-- well, this of course came out after the war-- the Kastner Affair that you must have heard about, when Rudolf Kastner was accused of not telling the Hungarian Jewry about things that apparently he knew about.
I-- you know, I don't want to take sides in that issue.

But, but the fact is that Hungarian Jews and, certainly, Jews in, in the provinces other than the capital, were quite ignorant about what was going on in, in Poland with the Jews in, in Nazi-occupied Europe and other places like in the Baltic states.
And by the time I think they realized, it was too late.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father, till today, I think, he feels guilty about it.
He's got a tremendous guilt for not doing something about it when there was still time to do something about it.
But I can't blame him.
He didn't know.
He-- the, the, the, the-- we were-- we were assimilated Jews.
Certainly, that part of Hungary, the part that's called Transdanubia, most Jews were pretty assimilated to the Hungarian population and Hungarian culture.
My father was actually an officer in the First World War in the Hungarian-- Austro-Hungarian Army and fought, of course, on the side of Hungary against the Allies.
Hungary always had a knack of joining the wrong side in history.
It's a-- it's a tremendous knack.
And, and so he felt that nothing could be done to him or to his family or Jews who were-- who felt so Hungarian and so-- so patriotic, if you like.
No, I mean, there were-- there were some covert discrimination, I suppose.
But nothing-- no, there was no overt anti-Semitism or-- until quite late.
I mean, he, he went-- he, he, you know, he did very well in his business.
And he had a lot of Christian clients.
I mean, it wasn't like in Germany, where, at some point, people were discouraged from buying-- you know, going to Jewish stores and deal with Jews.
No, he had a lot of Christian clients and customers.
And no, he was-- he was doing all right.

I mean, we didn't have-- I mean, the fact that I-- we went to a, a Catholic school, I think, even as, as external students, or special students, attest to the fact that, you know, Jews were tolerated, if nothing else.
And there were no-- there was no overt discrimination until, until, until the German invasion.
Well, of course, that was in 1940-- March 19, 1944 when the Germans invaded-- essentially, invaded Hungary.
And, and they took over, more or less.
And not just them, but the faction of, of, of the Hungarian government, or the Hungarian parties, that were pro-Nazi took over.
And things changed very rapidly.
Within weeks, I think, we had to start wearing the yellow Star of David.
It was the law.
I mean, I, I think one day, it's just-- it was proclaimed as the law.
And, and I recall I was probably 10 years old or thereabouts-- was it-- yeah, it was 1944.
I was 10 or 11 years old.
And, and one morning, my father just told us that from now on we have to wear those yellow Stars of David whenever we go any place.
And it will have to be sewn on the-- on, on our clothing.
And that's it.
And so it, it was the law.
I mean, it wasn't a-- I'm not sure how parliament enacted the law.
But it was the law.
I mean, it wasn't-- there's no question about it.
And that was just the beginning.
And then, of course, a few weeks later, or a couple of months later-- this was in March-- and by May, we were actually herded into a ghetto.
Now, Pecs never had a ghetto.
I mean, it wasn't the kind of town where Jews were concentrated in any one single area.
And so there was no ghetto in Pecs.
And what the authorities did, actually, they, they designated a certain area which was a, a, a lower-class, working-class area.
And they said, well, that's going to be the ghetto.
And everybody from that area, the workers, would have to leave, vacate the premises.
Jews will move into those area-- those flats and apartments and houses.
And, and, of course, the workers that were vacated from there will take the, the, the apartments and houses of the Jews.
And so they, they artificially designated-- they created a ghetto.
And--
Well, I can-- I can-- I can't really testify to what the general feeling was.
I, I, I don't-- I don't recall.
But there were some people who were quite happy to see the Jews [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sort of herded into a ghetto.
And others probably were, were more sympathetic to the Jews.
I, I don't-- I can't recall any particular instance.
At that point, I wasn't really concerned with what they felt.
We were just trying to, to survive and to make sure that-- see how much we can take over to, to the ghetto.
Because all we got-- I mean, we had a house.
We had a, a, a, a fairly substantial house before the war and early part of the war.
And when we had to move to that ghetto, we-- all we got was one room.
Now, at that point, my father wasn't with us anymore because he had been taken away to what was called a labor brigade or whatever they-- I'm not sure how to translate it.
It was like labor service, where all able-bodied men were actually called up.
And they weren't necessarily on the front, but they were assisting Hungarian Army in the war effort with menial jobs and, and that type of thing.
I mean, they weren't fighting.
They weren't trusted to fight.
But, but they were doing menial jobs for, for the army and the Hungarian forces.
So all the able-bodied men were taken away, including my father.
And that, of course, eliminated any possibility of resistance, which was, of course, the primary purpose of it is-- I mean, if the men are not there, who's going to rebel?
And who's going to fight?
So at that point, only my mother and the three children-- my sister and my brother and I, four of us-- were in, in-- sort of taken to the ghetto and, and put in one room.
OK, we'll pause there.
And we'll pick up on the next tape.
Oh, OK.
